report: Blank stare, followed by rightward head version and right arm extension, subsequently evolving into bilateral tonic-clonic movements with rhythmic jerking of both arms and legs, consistent with a generalized tonic-clonic seizure.

The patient initially presents with a blank stare or transient gaze, followed by rightward head version and extension of the right arm. In some instances, the right arm exhibits initial flexion before progressing to tonic stiffening. Tonic phase involvement includes unilateral (right-sided) limb rigidity that may evolve into bilateral tonic stiffening. This is subsequently followed by clonic jerks involving both arms and legs, indicating progression to a bilateral convulsive seizure. The eyes remain open throughout the events, and there is no loss of consciousness in certain episodes, while in others the patient remains unresponsive from onset through the postictal period. One atypical segment describes a leftward head turn with left arm flexion and right arm extension in a supine patient without clonic activity or impaired awareness. Overall, the majority of episodes feature a consistent pattern of altered awareness, lateralizing signs to the right side, and secondary generalization with bilateral rhythmic motor activity.

Patient initially exhibits a blank stare, followed by rightward head version and right arm extension. Subsequently, bilateral tonic-clonic movements occur, characterized by rhythmic jerking of both arms and legs. The patient remains unresponsive throughout the seizure.

Patient in supine position, head slightly turned left, right arm extended outward, left arm resting on bed or flexed. Initially unresponsive with a blank stare, followed by rightward head version and right arm extension. Subsequently, bilateral tonic-clonic activity ensues with clonic jerks involving both arms and legs, exhibiting asynchronous shaking. No obvious automatisms observed throughout the sequence.

Patient initially shows a blank stare, followed by rightward head version and right arm extension. Subsequently, the patient exhibits tonic stiffening, with the left arm flexed and the right arm extended, followed by bilateral rhythmic jerking of both arms and legs, indicative of clonic jerks. The patient shifts from a supine position to a left lateral position during the event and remains unresponsive throughout the seizure.

The patient initially presented with a blank stare and brief period of unresponsiveness, followed by rightward head version and right arm extension. Throughout the early phase, the eyes remained closed, and while lying in bed appearing at rest, there was gradual extension of the left arm, which occurred alongside relative stillness of the right arm prior to its involvement.

The patient remains in a supine position, covered with a white blanket or sheet. Initially, there are no observable early signs of a seizure such as lip smacking or unilateral arm movements, and the patient appears to be resting quietly without tonic stiffening or clonic jerks. At one point, the patient is holding a phone in their left hand and gesturing with their right hand, which has a bandage on the wrist; their facial expression changes from neutral to one of discomfort as they rub their face with the right hand—no seizure activity is evident during this time. Subsequently, the patient exhibits a blank stare followed by rightward head version and right arm extension. The right arm shows tonic stiffening that transitions into clonic jerks, while the left arm demonstrates slight flexion. The patient's eyes remain closed throughout the event, and they remain unresponsive during the seizure.
